es.meaning: To be a bit crazy; said jokingly or affectionately.
es.example: Mi vecino está como una cabra, pone música a las tres de la mañana.
es.exampleTranslation: My neighbor's nuts, he blasts music at 3am.
es.regionNote: STRONGLY PENINSULAR. Rare in Mexico — a Mexican speaker would more likely say 'está zafado' or 'está loco de remate'. Needs a LatAm equivalent before shipping to a LatAm-default audience.

it.literal: to be out like a balcony
it.meaning: To be out of one's mind.
it.example: Ma sei fuori come un balcone? Non puoi guidare così!
it.exampleTranslation: Are you out of your mind? You can't drive like that!
it.regionNote: Colloquial/regional (strong in Emilia and Rome). NOT in Treccani. Fine among friends; wrong for a CILS oral.

bridge.note: Spanish reaches for an animal, Italian for architecture. The Italian is a pun: a balcony juts fuori ('out'), and essere fuori alone already means to be out of one's mind.

review.notes: THE CAUTIONARY ENTRY. Shipped unflagged in the personal app. IT side is slang and dictionary-absent (source is a slang dictionary, mapped to authority 'Corpus'); ES side is Peninsular-only. Both problems must be resolved, not just documented. 2026-07-17: Europass list surfaced 'essere una capra' (IT) = to be IGNORANT — a false friend of this entry's ES side (como una cabra = crazy). Same goat, different defect. Consider a cross-language note or companion entry.
